title: Risotto alla zucca
subtitle: Butternut squash risotto with sage and Parmesan
excerpt: Butternut squash risotto in the Venetian style: half the squash simmered into the stock for colour and sweetness, the other half diced and added midway so it keeps some texture. Finished with crisp sage leaves fried in butter. Golden, sweet-savoury, and exactly right for autumn.
sourceNotes: Risotto alla zucca is a Venetian and Mantuan preparation strongly associated with autumn harvests in the Po valley. Artusi covers risotto with vegetables and autumn squash preparations in La scienza in cucina (1891, public domain); the pumpkin and squash version of risotto is documented in regional Venetian cookbooks from the late nineteenth century. Crown prince, onion squash, or delica pumpkin can substitute for butternut.

glossaryTerms.definition: The final step of risotto-making: cold butter and grated cheese beaten vigorously into the hot rice off the heat. The rapid agitation emulsifies the fat with the starchy cooking liquid, creating the characteristic creamy, flowing texture of a properly made risotto.

content.text: The trick with squash risotto is splitting the squash into two jobs. Half of it goes into the stock: simmered until soft, then blended into the cooking liquid, which turns it deep orange and builds the squash flavour into every grain of rice from the start. The other half is diced small and stirred in midway through, giving the finished risotto something to bite into.

The sage butter is not optional. Fry whole sage leaves in butter until crisp and the butter turns nutty brown, then spoon both over each serving. The bitter, aromatic sage cuts the sweetness of the squash in a way that nothing else quite does.

items.prepNote: peeled and deseeded weight; split 500 g for stock, 400 g diced 1 cm
20 g for cooking, 40 g cold for mantecatura, 20 g for sage butter

content.text: Put 500 g of the {{butternut-squash}} (chopped roughly) into a saucepan with the {{stock-vegetable}} of vegetable stock. Bring to a simmer and cook for 15 minutes until the squash is completely soft. Blend until smooth, or mash well and press through a sieve. Season with salt. You should have about 1 litre of thick, orange cooking liquid. Keep hot over the lowest heat.

Warm the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil and 20 g of the {{unsalted-butter}} in a wide, heavy pan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and cook for 8 minutes until soft and pale gold at the edges. Add the {{arborio-rice}} of risotto rice and stir for 2 minutes until the grains look translucent at the edges.

Pour in the {{white-wine-dry}} of white wine. Stir until absorbed. Add the squash stock a ladleful at a time, stirring constantly and waiting for each addition to absorb before adding the next. After 10 minutes, stir in the remaining 400 g of diced squash. Continue adding stock for another 8 to 10 minutes until the rice is cooked through with a faint bite. If you run out of stock, use hot water.
Finish with mantecatura and sage butter
Remove from the heat. Add 40 g of cold butter in pieces and the {{parmesan}} of Parmesan. Beat vigorously for 60 seconds: this is the

. The risotto should be glossy and flow slowly when the pan is tilted. Taste for salt and pepper. Cover and rest for 2 minutes. Meanwhile, melt the remaining 20 g of butter in a small pan over medium-high heat. Add the {{sage}} leaves and fry for 30 to 60 seconds until crisp and the butter turns pale brown. Serve the risotto in warm bowls, spoon the sage butter over each portion.

items.symptom: Risotto is too sweet with no contrast
items.cause: Sage butter skipped, or squash very sweet (some varieties are sweeter than others)
items.fix: The sage butter is essential for balance; do not skip it; a pinch of black pepper in the risotto also helps

items.cause: Added too early; diced squash needs only 8 to 10 minutes
items.fix: Add the diced squash only at the halfway point of cooking; it should be tender but still hold its shape

items.cause: Overcooked, or too little stock, or mantecatura done over heat
items.fix: Remove from heat before the rice is fully done; mantecatura happens off the heat with cold butter

items.symptom: Sage leaves went soggy rather than crisping
items.cause: Butter not hot enough, or too many leaves crowded the pan
items.fix: Fry the sage in hot foaming butter; leaves should sizzle immediately; fry in batches if needed

content.text: Risotto alla zucca belongs to the autumn table of Lombardy and the Veneto, where squash has been grown since its introduction from the Americas in the sixteenth century. The combination of sweet squash, sage, and butter is one of the canonical flavour groupings of the Italian north, appearing across pasta, risotto, and gnocchi preparations. Mantua is particularly associated with squash as a filling for tortelli di zucca; the risotto version is a simpler expression of the same pairing, requiring no pasta-making and considerably less time.
